lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: Nurse, where’s my daughter? call her forth to me.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Now, by my maidenhead, at twelve year old,

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: I bade her come. What, lamb! what, lady-bird!

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: God forbid! Where’s this girl? What, Juliet!

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: How now! who calls?

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Your mother.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Madam, I am here.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: What is your will?

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: This is the matter:—Nurse, give leave awhile,

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: We must talk in secret:—nurse, come back again;

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: I have remember’d me, thou’s hear our counsel.

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: Thou know’st my daughter’s of a pretty age.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Faith, I can tell her age unto an hour.

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: She’s not fourteen.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: I’ll lay fourteen of my teeth—

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: And yet, to my teen be it spoken, I have but four—

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: She is not fourteen. How long is it now

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: To Lammas-tide?

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: A fortnight and odd days.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Even or odd, of all days in the year,

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Come Lammas-eve at night shall she be fourteen.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Susan and she—God rest all Christian souls!—

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Were of an age: well, Susan is with God;

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: She was too good for me: but, as I said,

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: On Lammas-eve at night shall she be fourteen;

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: That shall she, marry; I remember it well.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: ’Tis since the earthquake now eleven years;

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: And she was wean’d—I never shall forget it—

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Of all the days of the year, upon that day:

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: For I had then laid wormwood to my dug,

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Sitting in the sun under the dove-house wall;

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: My lord and you were then at Mantua:—

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Nay, I do bear a brain:—but, as I said,

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: When it did taste the wormwood on the nipple

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Of my dug and felt it bitter, pretty fool,

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: To see it tetchy and fall out with the dug!

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: “Shake” quoth the dove-house: ’twas no need, I trow,

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: To bid me trudge:

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: And since that time it is eleven years;

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: For then she could stand alone; nay, by the rood,

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: She could have run and waddled all about;

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: For even the day before, she broke her brow:

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: And then my husband—God be with his soul!

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: A’ was a merry man—took up the child:

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: “Yea,” quoth he, “dost thou fall upon thy face?

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Thou wilt fall backward when thou hast more wit;

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Wilt thou not, Jule?” and, by my holidame,

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: The pretty wretch left crying and said “Ay.”

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: To see, now, how a jest shall come about!

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: I warrant, an I should live a thousand years,

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: I never should forget it: “Wilt thou not, Jule?” quoth he;

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: And, pretty fool, it stinted and said “Ay.”

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: Enough of this; I pray thee, hold thy peace.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Yes, madam: yet I cannot choose but laugh,

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: To think it should leave crying and say “Ay.”

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: And yet, I warrant, it had upon its brow

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: A bump as big as a young cockerel’s stone;

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: A parlous knock; and it cried bitterly:

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: “Yea,” quoth my husband, “fall’st upon thy face?

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Thou wilt fall backward when thou comest to age;

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Wilt thou not, Jule?” it stinted and said “Ay.”

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: And stint thou too, I pray thee, nurse, say I.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Peace, I have done. God mark thee to his grace!

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Thou wast the prettiest babe that e’er I nursed:

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: An I might live to see thee married once,

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: I have my wish.

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: Marry, that “marry” is the very theme

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: I came to talk of. Tell me, daughter Juliet,

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: How stands your disposition to be married?

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: It is an honour that I dream not of.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: An honour! were not I thine only nurse,

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: I would say thou hadst suck’d wisdom from thy teat.

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: Well, think of marriage now; younger than you,

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: Here in Verona, ladies of esteem,

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: Are made already mothers: by my count,

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: I was your mother much upon these years

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: That you are now a maid. Thus then in brief:

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: The valiant Paris seeks you for his love.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: A man, young lady! lady, such a man

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: As all the world—why, he’s a man of wax.

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: Verona’s summer hath not such a flower.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Nay, he’s a flower; in faith, a very flower.

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: What say you? can you love the gentleman?

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: This night you shall behold him at our feast;

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: Read o’er the volume of young Paris’ face,

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: And find delight writ there with beauty’s pen;

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: Examine every married lineament,

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: And see how one another lends content,

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: And what obscured in this fair volume lies

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: Find written in the margent of his eyes.

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: This precious book of love, this unbound lover,

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: To beautify him, only lacks a cover:

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: The fish lives in the sea, and ’tis much pride

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: For fair without the fair within to hide:

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: That book in many’s eyes doth share the glory,

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: That in gold clasps locks in the golden story;

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: So shall you share all that he doth possess,

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: By having him, making yourself no less.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: No less! nay, bigger; women grow by men.

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: Speak briefly, can you like of Paris’ love?

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: I’ll look to like, if looking liking move:

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: But no more deep will I endart mine eye

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Than your consent gives strength to make it fly.

lines.speaker: SERVANT
lines.text: Madam, the guests are come, supper served up, you called, my young

lines.speaker: SERVANT
lines.text: lady asked for, the nurse cursed in the pantry, and everything in

lines.speaker: SERVANT
lines.text: extremity. I must hence to wait; I beseech you, follow straight.

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: We follow thee. Juliet, the county stays.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Go, girl, seek happy nights to happy days.

glosses.definition: allow us privacy; leave us
Juliet is explicitly under fourteen — the play makes her extreme youth textually unavoidable
August 1st, a harvest festival — Juliet's birthday falls two weeks before, on July 31
July 31 — Juliet's fourteenth birthday is still two weeks away

glosses.phrase: Susan and she—God rest all Christian souls!
glosses.definition: the Nurse had a daughter Susan, who died; her grief fuels her bond to Juliet
the Nurse nursed Juliet — timeline hint for the Nurse's intimate bond
a bitter herb applied to the nipple to repel a nursing child
nipple; teat
peevish; fretful
dovecote; pigeon-house
walk with weary effort
cross of Christ — mild oath
Juliet's baby-name
stopped; ceased

glosses.phrase: God mark thee to his grace
glosses.definition: God take you into his favor — blessing
the Nurse's memory of Juliet as an infant
holy relic or lady — mild oath
cease
inclination; mood
a great honor — Lady Capulet's word for marriage
a figure as perfectly moulded as a wax sculpture — flawless
the finest example

glosses.phrase: read o’er the volume of young Paris’ face
glosses.definition: Lady Capulet sustains a conceit of Paris as a book to be read
well-matched feature
each feature complements the next
unbound as a book — also a pun on unmarried
proverbial — fish naturally belongs in water, as a man in his proper element

annotations.title: Juliet's age — not yet fourteen
annotations.body: The play states Juliet's age unmistakably: she is thirteen, two weeks short of fourteen. Modern readers encounter this as disturbing, and the play does not soften it. Three contexts should be held together. First, Elizabethan aristocratic betrothal could occur young — the legal marriage age was twelve for girls, fourteen for boys, though actual marriage typically occurred in the late teens or twenties even among the nobility (Shakespeare's own wife Anne Hathaway was 26 when he married her at 18). Second, Shakespeare changed his source: Arthur Brooke's poem The Tragicall Historye of Romeus and Juliet (1562) makes Juliet sixteen; Shakespeare deliberately lowers her age. Third, the choice is dramatically freighted — Juliet's extreme youth sharpens the tragedy of her agency. She does not merely suffer events; she decides. A thirteen-year-old who insists on marrying, contrives her own fake death, and chooses suicide by her husband's side makes the play's claim about love's sovereignty more radical, not less disturbing.
sources: Arthur Brooke, The Tragicall Historye of Romeus and Juliet (1562)
Stephen Greenblatt, Will in the World (2004)
Lawrence Stone, The Family, Sex and Marriage in England 1500-1800 (1977)

annotations.title: The Nurse in prose — comic register among nobles
annotations.body: Lady Capulet speaks in verse; the Nurse speaks in prose. Her famous weaning-story ('When it did taste the wormwood on the nipple / Of my dug, and felt it bitter') is a class-marker as much as a comic showpiece. The Nurse's earthy physicality — her dead daughter Susan, her dead husband's joke, her specific memory of breast-feeding — gives Juliet the only character in the play who has touched her body with her own. That bond makes the Nurse's betrayal of Juliet in 3.5 the most painful break in Juliet's adolescence, more than her father's rage. Notice how the Nurse's prose resists Lady Capulet's repeated attempts to return to courtly verse: it is class and intimacy reasserting themselves against formality.
sources: Russ McDonald, Shakespeare and the Arts of Language (2001)

trials.prompt: How old is Juliet at the time of the play?
options: Sixteen, as in Shakespeare's source (Brooke's poem)
Twelve — the legal minimum marriage age in Elizabethan England
Thirteen — 'not yet fourteen,' with Lammas Eve (July 31) marking her fourteenth birthday two weeks off

trials.prompt: Shakespeare changed Juliet's age from sixteen (in his source, Brooke's poem) to thirteen. What is the most likely dramatic effect?
options: It makes the parents' urgency to marry her off seem excessive
It sharpens the tragedy — her extreme youth heightens both vulnerability and the force of her decisions
It makes her unable to legally marry
It reduces her to a passive victim

trials.prompt: Why does the Nurse speak in prose while Lady Capulet speaks in verse?
options: Prose marks her class and intimacy; verse marks Lady Capulet's formality
Prose indicates she's uneducated and therefore untrustworthy
All older women in Shakespeare speak in prose
